stem: Expand along the first row to compute \( \det\begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{pmatrix} \).
distractor_rationales: This uses all plus signs instead of the alternating \( +,-,+ \) cofactor pattern.
This uses all minus signs after the first term instead of the alternating pattern.
This is the 'rule of Sarrus' diagonal-product sum, but with the second triple of diagonals omitted.
This drops the middle cofactor term entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

solution: For an (upper) triangular matrix the determinant is just the product of the diagonal entries: \( ([[a]])([[d]])([[f]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This sums the diagonal entries (that gives the trace), not their product.
This incorrectly adds a contribution from the off-diagonal entries, which never affect a triangular determinant.
This has the right magnitude but the wrong sign.
This multiplies entries from the first row instead of the main diagonal.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: In \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[r31]] & [[r32]] & [[r33]] \end{pmatrix} \), row 3 equals \( [[k1]] \) times row 1 plus \( [[k2]] \) times row 2. What is \( \det(A) \)?
solution: Since row 3 is a linear combination of rows 1 and 2, the rows are linearly dependent, so \( \det(A) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the combination coefficient \( [[k1]] \) with the determinant.
This confuses the combination coefficient \( [[k2]] \) with the determinant.
This adds the two combination coefficients instead of recognizing the determinant vanishes.
This multiplies the two combination coefficients instead of recognizing the determinant vanishes.
A dependent set of rows never gives determinant 1 in general; the determinant is forced to 0.
A dependent set of rows never gives determinant -1 in general; the determinant is forced to 0.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) and let \( B \) be \( A \) with its first row multiplied by \( [[k]] \), i.e. \( B = \begin{pmatrix} [[ka]] & [[kb]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \). What is \( \det(B) \)?
solution: Scaling a single row of a matrix by \( [[k]] \) scales the determinant by that same factor: \( \det(B) = [[k]]\cdot\det(A) = [[k]]\cdot(([[a]])([[d]])-([[b]])([[c]])) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to scale at all and just gives \( \det(A) \).
This scales by \( k^2 \), which is what happens if BOTH rows were scaled by \( k \), not just one.
This adds \( k \) to the determinant instead of multiplying by it.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.
